learningGoal: Learn to shift letters forward through the alphabet like a codebreaker
Get confident with +1, +2, +3 shifts — you'll be doing these in your sleep!

screens.title: Can you crack the code for CAT?
screens.body:
Daisy is practising forward shifts.

Did you know? Spies and secret agents have used letter shifts for centuries! A **forward shift** means every letter jumps FORWARD in the alphabet. A shift of +2 means each letter moves 2 places forward.

Can you work out what **CAT** becomes?

screens.title: Let's count forward!
screens.body:
Let's work through **CAT** together with a shift of +2. Take each letter and count 2 places forward in the alphabet.

Top tip: use **EJOTY** (E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25) as landmarks to keep your place!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Note the shift, count forward, write the result. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by noting the shift amount first!

steps.why: Same shift applies to every letter

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Each letter shifts **forward 2**. What does **RUG** become?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Each letter shifts forward 2: R→T, U→W, G→I. RUG becomes TWI. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "TWI". Each letter shifts forward 2: R→T, U→W, G→I. RUG becomes TWI. ✓

screens.title: You've cracked forward shifts!
screens.body: Here's your recipe for any forward shift:

steps.text: 2. Take each letter and count forward
steps.why: Use EJOTY (a memory trick — every 5th letter: E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25) to stay on track

steps.text: 3. Write down the new letter each time

learningGoal: Spot the sneaky mistakes people make with forward shifts
Learn why off-by-one errors happen — and how to dodge them

screens.title: Is "DEF" the right code for BED?
screens.body:
Evie is checking her brother's code homework. The answer given was **"DEF"** as the +2 code for **BED**.

But that's wrong! Can you spot the mistake?

screens.title: Spot the counting error!
screens.body:
The mistake: counted the starting letter as step 1 instead of step 0.

Counting B as 1 gives C at 2 — but the shift should START after B. B +2 means go 2 MORE: B→C→D

steps.why: counted the starting letter as step 1 instead of step 0
Count forward from the starting letter — don't count the letter itself
Every letter must shift by the same amount

screens.title: What's the CORRECT code?
screens.body: Shift **MOP** forward by 2. What's the right answer?

interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! M +2 = O, O +2 = Q, P +2 = R. MOP becomes OQR. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "OQR". M +2 = O, O +2 = Q, P +2 = R. MOP becomes OQR. ✓

screens.title: Now you'll dodge these mistakes!
screens.body: Watch out for these common forward-shift traps:

steps.text: Don't count the starting letter as step 1
Shift EVERY letter — including first and last

learningGoal: Master backward shifts — the same skill, just in reverse
Apply -1, -2, -3 shifts without getting muddled

screens.title: Can you decode SUN backwards?
screens.body:
Daisy is practising backward shifts.

A **backward shift** means every letter moves BACK in the alphabet. A shift of -1 means each letter moves 1 place backward.

It's the same idea as forward shifts, just in the other direction. Can you work out what **SUN** becomes?

screens.title: Let's count backward!
screens.body:
Let's work through **SUN** together with a shift of -1. Take each letter and count 1 place back in the alphabet.

Top tip: use **EJOTY** (E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25) as landmarks to keep your place!

steps.why: Same shift applies to every letter

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Each letter shifts **backward 1**. What does **FOG** become?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Each letter shifts back 1: F→E, O→N, G→F. FOG becomes ENF. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "ENF". Each letter shifts back 1: F→E, O→N, G→F. FOG becomes ENF. ✓

screens.title: Backward shifts — sorted!
screens.body: Here's your recipe for any backward shift:

steps.text: 2. Take each letter and count backward
steps.why: Use EJOTY (E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25) landmarks in reverse

steps.text: 3. Write the new letter each time

learningGoal: Build speed with backward shifts at different levels
Get so confident counting backward that it feels automatic

screens.title: Can you shift PIG backward?
screens.body:
Evie is building speed with backward codes.

Backward shifts can feel trickier at first — but here's the good news: the method is exactly the same, just in the other direction! You've got this.

**PIG** with a shift of **-1** becomes...?

screens.title: Step through it
screens.body:
Let's shift **PIG** by -1. Take each letter one at a time and count 1 back.

Use **EJOTY** (E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25) as landmarks to keep your place!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Each letter shifts **-1**. What does **HEN** become?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Each letter shifts back 1: H→G, E→D, N→M. HEN becomes GDM. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "GDM". Each letter shifts back 1: H→G, E→D, N→M. HEN becomes GDM. ✓

screens.title: Backward shifts — easy peasy!
screens.body: Backward shifts work the same as forward — just the other way. Remember:

steps.text: Use EJOTY (E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25) landmarks in reverse
Take your time with each letter

learningGoal: Verify that every letter shifts by the SAME amount — the key to cracking any code
Learn the detective trick that confirms you've found the right rule

screens.title: What's the rule for GAME → HBNF?
screens.body:
Zara is verifying a code she's been given.

In the 11+, you're often given a coded pair and asked: **what rule was used?** It's like being a codebreaker!

The key is to check that EVERY letter shifts by the **same amount**. If they do, you've cracked it!

screens.title: Check every letter
screens.body: Let's check **GAME → HBNF**. Line up each original letter with its coded version and count the hops. They should ALL be the same number.

bodyParts.content: Let's check **GAME → HBNF**. Count the hops for each letter — they should ALL be the same!

steps.why: Count the hops between original and coded letter
Same number every time — that's the rule! ✓

screens.title: What's the shift rule?
screens.body: **GAME** is coded as **HBNF**. What shift has been used?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! G→H (+1), A→B (+1), M→N (+1), E→F (+1). Every letter shifts +1 — the rule is consistent. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "+1 (forward)". G→H (+1), A→B (+1), M→N (+1), E→F (+1). Every letter shifts +1 — the rule is consistent. ✓

screens.title: You're a codebreaker now!
screens.body: To identify the shift rule, just follow these steps:

steps.text: 1. Line up original and coded letters
2. Count the hops for EACH pair
3. They should all be the SAME number
steps.why: If they match, that's the rule! ✓

learningGoal: Handle trickier codes where each position shifts by a different amount
Spot patterns like +1, +2, +3 hiding inside position-based shifts

screens.title: Something's different about this code!
screens.body:
Daisy is tackling a tricky variable-shift code.

Here's a twist: sometimes the shift is **different for each position** in the word. The first letter might shift +1, the second +2, the third +3... There's a pattern hiding in the shifts themselves!

**CAT** → **DCW**

Can you spot it?

screens.title: Check each position separately
screens.body: Let's check **CAT → DCW**. When the shift ISN'T consistent (the same for every letter), look for a pattern in the shift amounts themselves:

steps.why: Count the hops for each letter separately
Different shift amount — the shifts vary!
Different shift amount — the shifts vary!

steps.text: Position 1 shifts +1, position 2 shifts +2, position 3 shifts +3
steps.why: The shifts follow their own pattern! ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The shifts are **+1, +2, +3**. What does **CAT** become?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Each position shifts differently: C+1=D, A+2=C, T+3=W. The pattern is +1, +2, +3. CAT becomes DCW. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "DCW". Each position shifts differently: C+1=D, A+2=C, T+3=W. The pattern is +1, +2, +3. CAT becomes DCW. ✓

screens.title: Variable shifts — you can handle these!
screens.body: When each position shifts differently, just follow this approach:

steps.text: 1. Count the hops for each letter separately
steps.why: Don't assume they're all the same!

steps.text: 2. Look for a pattern in the shifts
3. Apply the pattern to the new word
steps.why: Each position gets its own shift amount ✓

learningGoal: Master the EJOTY speed trick — your secret weapon for finding letter positions fast
Never count from A again — jump to the nearest landmark instead!

screens.title: EJOTY — your secret weapon!
screens.body:
Daisy is learning the EJOTY speed trick.

Did you know? Top scorers in the 11+ almost never count from A! They use **EJOTY** (E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25) — 5 anchor points spaced evenly through the alphabet.

Jump to the nearest landmark and count from there. It's so much faster!

screens.title: Finding R with EJOTY
screens.body:
The nearest EJOTY landmark to **R** is **T** = 20.

**R** is 2 before T, so R = **18**.

steps.text: What position is R in the alphabet?
steps.why: Instead of counting from A, use the nearest EJOTY landmark!
Jump straight to the closest EJOTY letter
Much faster than counting from A! ✓

screens.title: What position is this letter?
screens.body: Using EJOTY, what is the position of **M** in the alphabet?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! M is 2 before O(15), so M = 15 - 2 = 13. EJOTY makes it quick! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "13". M is 2 before O(15), so M = 15 - 2 = 13. EJOTY makes it quick! ✓

screens.title: EJOTY — learn it, love it!
screens.body: Once you've memorised these 5 landmarks, letter codes become so much faster:

steps.text: Find the nearest landmark to your letter
steps.why: Never more than 2 away from a landmark!

steps.text: Count a small number from there

learningGoal: Work backward from a coded word to reveal the hidden original
Discover the golden rule: decoding means doing the OPPOSITE shift

screens.title: What word is hiding inside "EPH"?
screens.body:
Daisy is working backward to find the original word.

Sometimes the 11+ gives you the **coded word** and asks: "What was the ORIGINAL word?" It's like uncovering a hidden message!

This is called **decoding** (turning the code back into a real word). The trick: if the code used **+1**, you decode by doing the **opposite**: **-1**.

screens.title: Reverse the shift!
screens.body: The code used a shift of **+1**. To decode, apply the opposite shift: **-1**.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Find the shift, reverse it, then check the result. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by noting the original shift, then reverse it!

screens.title: Decode it!
screens.body: The code **"CVT"** was made with a shift of **+1**. What was the original word?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The code used +1, so decode with -1: C→B, V→U, T→S. The original word is BUS. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "BUS". The code used +1, so decode with -1: C→B, V→U, T→S. The original word is BUS. ✓

screens.title: Decoding — you've got the golden rule!
screens.body: To find the original word from a code, just remember:

steps.text: 1. Note the shift that was used
3. The result should spell a real word!
steps.why: If it doesn't make a word, recheck your working ✓

learningGoal: Decode coded words back to their originals — like cracking a secret message
Reverse both forward and backward shifts with confidence

screens.title: What word is hiding in "VQRZ"?
screens.body:
Evie is decoding a weather word.

The coded word is **"VQRZ"** and the shift used was **+3**.

Remember: to decode, do the **opposite** — shift each letter by **-3**.

screens.title: Apply the opposite shift
screens.body: The code used **+3**, so to decode we shift each letter by **-3**:

bodyParts.content: The code used **+3**, so to decode we shift each letter by **-3**:

screens.title: Decode it!
screens.body: The code is **"KLOO"** (shift: +3). What is the original word?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Decode with -3: K→H, L→I, O→L, O→L. The word is HILL. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "HILL". Decode with -3: K→H, L→I, O→L, O→L. The word is HILL. ✓

screens.title: Decoding — just flip the shift!
screens.body: Remember the golden rule — it's beautifully simple:

steps.text: Check: does your answer spell a real word?

subConcepts.name: Wrap-Around — Past Z or Before A

learningGoal: Handle the cool trick when a shift goes past Z or before A — the alphabet loops!
Count through the wrap-around join like a pro

screens.title: What happens AFTER Z?
screens.body:
Daisy is handling a shift that goes past Z.

Here's a fun one: sometimes a shift takes you **past Z** or **before A**. When this happens, the alphabet **wraps around** — imagine bending the alphabet into a circle!

• After Z comes A (Z → A → B → C...)
• Before A comes Z (A → Z → Y → X...)

Once you picture it as a loop, it makes total sense!

screens.title: Count through the wrap
screens.body: Just keep counting as if the alphabet loops around. Y is position 25. Adding 3 goes past Z(26), so it wraps: Y→Z(1 hop)→A(2 hops)→B(3 hops)

interaction.sentence: When a shift goes past Z, the alphabet ____ back to A and keeps counting
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! The alphabet wraps around — after Z comes A, and before A comes Z. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — the alphabet wraps around, like a circle!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Shift **YEW** by **+2**. Watch out for wrap-arounds!

interaction.question: What is YEW shifted by +2?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Y+2 wraps past Z to A, E+2=G, W+2=Y. YEW becomes AGY. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "AGY". Y+2 wraps past Z to A, E+2=G, W+2=Y. YEW becomes AGY. ✓

screens.title: Wrap-around — you've got it!
screens.body: When shifts go past Z or before A, just keep counting:

steps.text: After Z comes A, then B, then C...
steps.why: The alphabet loops back to the start

steps.text: Before A comes Z, then Y, then X...
steps.why: The alphabet loops back to the end

steps.text: Just keep counting — don't stop at Z or A!

learningGoal: Crack the shift rule from a given example pair — like a real codebreaker
Apply the rule you've discovered to encode a brand new word

screens.title: STAR → TUBS — what's the rule?
screens.body:
Daisy is finding the rule then applying it.

You're given **STAR → TUBS** and asked to apply the SAME rule to **MOON**.

Step 1: figure out the rule. Step 2: apply it!

screens.title: Find the rule, then apply it
screens.body: Let's crack **STAR → TUBS**. Check each letter to find the shift, then apply it to **MOON**:

bodyParts.content: First, find the rule from **STAR → TUBS**. Then apply it to **MOON**:

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Guess, verify, then apply. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always guess the rule first, then verify before applying!

screens.title: Apply the rule!
screens.body: If **STAR** is coded as **TUBS**, what is **MOON** coded as?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! STAR→TUBS is +1. Apply +1 to MOON: M→N, O→P, O→P, N→O = NPPO. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "NPPO". STAR→TUBS is +1. Apply +1 to MOON: M→N, O→P, O→P, N→O = NPPO. ✓

screens.title: Spot it, check it, apply it!
screens.body: You've now got the complete strategy for coded-pair questions:

steps.text: 1. Check the first letter pair to guess the shift

steps.why: Make sure EVERY letter shifts the same

steps.text: 3. Apply that exact shift to the new word

learningGoal: Crack shift rules from trickier example pairs at speed
Build your pattern-spotting skills so they become lightning fast

screens.title: Can you crack DESK → GHVN?
screens.body:
Evie is racing to spot the pattern.

Look at the example pair and work out the rule as FAST as you can. Then apply it!

**DESK** → **GHVN**

What's the rule? And what does **LAMP** become?

screens.title: The rule is +3
screens.body: Let's confirm the rule for **DESK → GHVN**, then apply it to **LAMP**:

bodyParts.content: Confirm the rule from **DESK → GHVN**, then apply it to **LAMP**:

interaction.sentence: To spot the rule quickly, check the ____ letter pair first, then verify with the last pair
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Start with the first letter pair — one pair is enough to guess the rule. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always start with the first letter pair to guess the rule!

screens.title: Apply the rule!
screens.body: **DESK** → **GHVN**. Using the same rule, what is **LAMP** coded as?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! DESK→GHVN is +3. Apply +3 to LAMP: L→O, A→D, M→P, P→S = ODPS. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "ODPS". DESK→GHVN is +3. Apply +3 to LAMP: L→O, A→D, M→P, P→S = ODPS. ✓

screens.title: You're getting faster at this!
screens.body: To spot the rule quickly in the exam:

steps.text: Start with the FIRST letter pair
steps.why: One pair is enough to guess the rule
Confirms your guess in one step
